course.title: EV Awareness & Safety
course.subtitle: Master the fundamentals of electric vehicle identification, high-voltage hazards, safety equipment, and emergency response procedures.

components.body: Your essential guide to identifying electric vehicles, understanding high-voltage hazards, and working safely in the modern automotive workplace.
components.imagePrompt: Modern electric vehicle in a sleek automotive workshop, dramatic lighting with electric blue (#0099ff) accent glow, dark atmospheric background (#383838), cinematic wide-angle composition, professional photography, high resolution, no text, no watermarks

_graphic.alt: Electric vehicle in a modern workshop with dramatic blue lighting

components.body:
This course is designed to improve your knowledge of Electric Vehicles (EVs) and make you aware of the associated risks, so that you may exercise appropriate care for yourself and others when working near an Electric Vehicle.

Important: This first-level course does not qualify you to work directly on high-voltage systems. You should inform your colleagues of your current trained level and request further assistance from a suitably trained person when necessary.

components.instruction: Explore each objective to understand what you'll learn.

_items.title: Know About EV Types
_items.body: Identify electric and hybrid vehicles currently available. Recognise alternative fuel source vehicles and understand the differences between BEV, HEV, and PHEV.

_items.title: Understand High-Voltage Hazards
_items.body: State the hazards associated with high-energy electricity. Understand the risks that may be present when connecting and charging electric/hybrid vehicles.

_items.title: Work Safely Around EVs
_items.body: State safety precautions to take before approaching and working around electric/hybrid vehicles. Identify high-energy cabling and associated components.

_items.title: Safe Charging Procedures
_items.body: Understand how vehicles may be safely charged using an external source. Know the correct procedures and terminology for EV charging.

components.body: Please enter your details before continuing.

It is critical that you can recognise an electric vehicle when it enters your workplace. Identifying features will differ between manufacturers, so you may need to exercise your skill and experience to determine the vehicle type — getting it wrong could be dangerous.

Look for these key exterior features:

  • Number plate: Full EVs may have a green border (not mandatory)
  • Badging: Many EVs display a badge indicating electric power
  • Charge port: BEVs and PHEVs have a battery charge connector port on the vehicle exterior
components.imagePrompt: Close-up of electric vehicle exterior showing green number plate and charging port, clean professional photography, blue (#0099ff) accent lighting, dark background, modern automotive style, no text, no watermarks

_graphic.alt: Electric vehicle exterior showing green number plate and charging port

components.body:
When inspecting the engine bay, look for these indicators:

  • Orange high-voltage cables — the universal indicator of an EV electrical system
  • Power controller / inverter — converts battery DC to motor AC
  • Warning labels — high-voltage warnings on components and covers
components.imagePrompt: Electric vehicle engine bay showing orange high-voltage cables and inverter components, clean technical photography, blue (#0099ff) and pink (#ff3c71) accent highlights, dark background, professional automotive style, no text, no watermarks

_graphic.alt: EV engine bay with orange high-voltage cables and warning labels

components.instruction: Review each indicator you would check when identifying an EV.

_items.detail: Look for EV, Electric, e-tron, or similar manufacturer badges.

_items.text: Power flow / energy use screen on dashboard

_items.detail: Not mandatory — do not rely on this alone.

components.body: Not all types of EV will have the same identifying features. A Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV) may not have a charging port, but it still contains high-voltage components that present serious risks.
Understanding the differences between vehicle types is essential for assessing risk and applying the correct procedures.

_items.title: BEV — Battery Electric Vehicle
_items.body: Fully electric, powered entirely by a rechargeable battery pack. No internal combustion engine. Charged exclusively via external mains connection.

_items.title: HEV — Hybrid Electric Vehicle
_items.body: Combines a conventional engine with an electric motor. The battery charges through regenerative braking — there is no external charging port.

_items.title: PHEV — Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle
_items.body: Has both a combustion engine and an electric motor with a larger battery than HEV. Can be charged via external mains connection for extended electric-only range.

imagePrompts.prompt: Modern battery electric vehicle (BEV) with charging cable connected, sleek design, blue (#0099ff) accent lighting, dark studio background, professional automotive photography, no text, no watermarks
Hybrid electric vehicle (HEV) cutaway showing dual engine and electric motor, technical illustration style, blue and pink accent colors, dark background, clean modern design, no text, no watermarks
Plug-in hybrid vehicle (PHEV) at charging station with visible charging port, modern professional photography, blue (#0099ff) glow accents, dark atmospheric background, no text, no watermarks

components.body: Different vehicle types can be identified by different features. Use this table as a quick reference.
You can use the UK Government Vehicle Enquiry website to check a vehicle's fuel type using its registration number. Which of the following is the correct URL?

_feedback.correct: Correct! The Government Vehicle Enquiry Service at vehicleenquiry.service.gov.uk allows you to check a vehicle's fuel type using its registration number.

_incorrect.final: Not quite. The correct website is vehicleenquiry.service.gov.uk — bookmark it for quick vehicle identification.

components.body: High-voltage systems in EVs present unique risks that require specific knowledge and precautions.
components.imagePrompt: Panoramic view of high-voltage warning signs and orange cables in an automotive workshop, dramatic dark atmosphere with electric blue (#0099ff) warning glow, ultra-wide cinematic composition, professional photography, no text, no watermarks

_graphic.alt: High-voltage warning signs and orange cables in automotive workshop

components.body: Electric and hybrid vehicles introduce hazards into the workplace in addition to those normally associated with vehicle repair and maintenance. Understanding these risks is the first step to working safely.

components.displayTitle: Key Risks of Working with EVs
components.instruction: Select each risk category to learn more.

_items.title: Fatal Electric Shock
_items.body: The presence of high-voltage components and cabling capable of delivering a fatal electric shock. Voltages in EVs can reach up to 650V DC — significantly higher than the 12/24V found in conventional vehicles. Components may retain dangerous voltage even when the vehicle is switched off.

_items.title: Fire & Explosion Risk
_items.body: The storage of electrical energy with the potential to cause explosion or fire. Stored electrical energy can be released suddenly if batteries are damaged or incorrectly modified, releasing explosive gases and harmful chemicals.

_items.title: Silent Operation
_items.body: Electric motors operate silently, meaning people may be unaware of vehicles moving. Most EVs are fitted with a pedestrian sound emitter, but this may not always be active in workshop environments.

_items.title: Unexpected Movement
_items.body: Electric motors can generate magnetic forces that cause unexpected movement. Remote operation keys that only need to be near the vehicle can accidentally power it up.

_items.title: Medical Device Interference
_items.body: High-voltage electrical systems may affect medical devices such as pacemakers. Staff with such devices should be made aware of this risk.

_items.title: Battery Handling
_items.body: Manual handling risks associated with battery replacement. Batteries are extremely heavy. Hybrid vehicles also retain a conventional fuel tank, adding further complexity.

_items.definition: The UK Health and Safety Executive provides specific guidance for working with electric and hybrid vehicles in the motor vehicle repair industry. Source: hse.gov.uk/mvr/topics/electric-hybrid.htm
A UN regulation that defines 'High Voltage' as >60V to ≤1500V DC (or >30V to ≤1000V AC). This is the standard used when referring to electric vehicle high-voltage systems.
The UK HSE identifies 4 categories of EV work: valeting/sales (low risk), incident response, routine maintenance, and high-voltage system work (specialist only).

components.body: Even for valeting, sales, and other lower-risk activities around EVs, these precautions must be followed:

_items.title: Secure the Key Fob
_items.body: Keep remote operation keys away from the vehicle to prevent accidental power-up. Use a signal-blocking pouch if available.

_items.title: Be Aware of Silent Movement
_items.body: People moving EVs around the workplace should be aware that others may not hear them approaching. Sound your horn when necessary.

_items.title: Avoid Pressure Washing HV Components
_items.body: High-pressure water can damage high-voltage electrical components and cables. Orange cables indicate HV systems — refer to manufacturer guidance before valeting in these areas.

_items.title: Never Touch Orange Cables
_items.body: Orange cables carry high voltage. Never handle, move, or lean tools against them. Report any visible damage immediately.

components.body:
A voltage as low as 50 volts applied between two parts of the human body causes a current to flow that can block the electrical signals between the brain and the muscles.

This may cause:

  • Stopping the heart beating properly
  • Preventing the person from breathing
  • Causing muscle spasms
  • Secondary injuries such as burns and fall injuries
components.imagePrompt: Technical diagram showing electrical safety concept with human body silhouette and voltage indicators, clean medical illustration style, blue (#0099ff) and pink (#ff3c71) accent elements, dark background, educational infographic, no text labels, no watermarks

_graphic.alt: Diagram illustrating the effects of electric shock on the human body

components.body: The severity of an electric shock depends on a combination of factors:

_items.title: Voltage Level
_items.body: EV systems operate at up to 650V DC — far exceeding the 50V threshold for danger. Higher voltage drives more current through the body.

_items.title: Current Path Through Body
_items.body: The path the current takes matters greatly. Hand-to-hand across the chest is the most dangerous. Finger-to-finger on the same hand is less severe but still harmful.

_items.title: Duration of Contact
_items.body: Even fractions of a second of contact with high voltage can be fatal. Muscle spasms may prevent the person from releasing their grip, prolonging exposure.

_items.title: Skin Conductivity
_items.body: Wet or sweaty skin dramatically reduces resistance, allowing more current to flow. Always ensure dry conditions when near HV components.

components.body: If you encounter someone receiving an electric shock, follow these steps in order:

_nodes.title: Assess the Situation
_nodes.body: Do NOT touch the casualty if they are still in contact with the electrical source. You are at risk of electrocution.

_nodes.title: Isolate the Power Source
_nodes.body: For mains chargers, turn off power at the isolation switch. For vehicle HV systems, turn off the vehicle using the normal key. Consider that the vehicle may not be safe even when switched off.

_nodes.title: Separate Casualty from Source
_nodes.body: If safe to do so, use a purpose-designed insulated rescue hook to separate the casualty from the power supply. Never use bare hands or conductive materials.

_nodes.title: Call 999
_nodes.body: Call emergency services immediately. Provide details about the high-voltage nature of the incident.

_nodes.title: Provide First Aid
_nodes.body: Once contact is broken, perform a primary survey and treat injuries. Seek assistance from trained first aiders on site.

_nodes.title: Secure the Scene
_nodes.body: Cordon off the vehicle using a non-conductive safety barrier chain. Ensure no one else attempts to work on it until it has been made safe by a specialist.

components.body: When electric vehicles enter your workplace, specific safety equipment must be available and correctly used. The following items should be present in any workshop or facility that handles EVs.

_items.title: Vehicle ID Sign
_items.body: Should be present on every electric vehicle entering a workshop for repair so that all staff are aware it is an EV. This is the primary visual indicator that special precautions apply.

_items.title: Barrier Chain
_items.body: A non-conductive safety barrier chain or cordon that alerts others and prevents unauthorised access to the EV work area. Must be non-conductive to prevent creating an additional electrical path.

_items.title: Key Signal Blocker
_items.body: A pouch or case that prevents accidental power-up on keyless-start vehicles by blocking the key from receiving and sending signals to the vehicle. Essential for preventing unexpected movement.

_items.title: Rescue Hook
_items.body: An insulated hook to be used by suitably trained staff in an emergency to remove a casualty from the source of an electric shock. Must be rated for the voltages present and regularly inspected.

_items.title: Insulated Gloves
_items.body: Class 0 insulated gloves rated to 1500V DC. Should be worn by suitably trained persons when working on the high-voltage system. Must be inspected before each use for tears or damage.

_items.title: Warning Signs
_items.body: Signs that alert others to the risks present in the EV work area. Includes 'Do Not Start / Work in Progress' signs to prevent unauthorised starting during repair.

components.body: What is the primary purpose of a key signal blocker pouch?

_items.text: Prevents the key from being stolen
Prevents accidental power-up on keyless-start vehicles by blocking key signals
Stops the key from being lost

_feedback.correct: Correct! The signal blocker prevents the vehicle from detecting the key and accidentally powering up, which could cause unexpected movement.

_incorrect.final: Not quite. The primary purpose is to block the key's signals, preventing the vehicle from accidentally powering up when someone is working near it.

components.body: What is the purpose of an insulated rescue hook?

_items.text: To be used by suitably trained staff in an emergency to remove a casualty from an electric shock source
To move high-voltage cables away from the car if they become short-circuited
To pull damaged battery packs out of the vehicle

_feedback.correct: Correct! The rescue hook is specifically designed to safely separate a person from an electrical source without the rescuer making direct contact.

_incorrect.final: The rescue hook's primary purpose is to safely remove a casualty from contact with an electrical source during an emergency.

components.body: To work safely around EV charging systems, you need to understand the basic electrical terminology. These concepts determine charging speed, cable ratings, and safety thresholds.
components.imagePrompt: Modern EV charging station with glowing cable and connector, sleek design with blue (#0099ff) LED indicators, dark atmospheric workshop background, professional photography, no text, no watermarks

_graphic.alt: Modern EV charging station with illuminated connector

components.instruction: Click each card to reveal the definition.

_items.back: Watts measure energy consumption — the rate at which electrical energy is used or generated. We're familiar with this from household items like lightbulbs. EV chargers are rated in kilowatts (kW).

Amps (amperes) measure electrical current — the flow of electricity through a circuit. There is a relationship between voltage, amps, and resistance. A higher amp reading means more current is flowing.

Voltage is electrical pressure — the force that pushes current through a circuit. ICE vehicles use 12V, trucks use 24V, but EVs operate at 400-650V DC. This is why EV electrical systems are so dangerous.
Kilowatt-hours measure battery capacity — how much energy the battery can store. A larger kWh rating means greater range. Typical EV batteries range from 40kWh to 100kWh.

_items.definition: A sudden release of electrical energy through the air when a high-voltage gap is breached. Can cause serious burns, injuries, or equipment damage.
Battery Electric Vehicle — powered entirely by a rechargeable battery pack with no internal combustion engine.
Hybrid Electric Vehicle — combines a conventional engine with an electric motor. Battery charges through regenerative braking only.
Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle — has both a combustion engine and electric motor with external charging capability.
The UN regulation defining high voltage as >60V to ≤1500V DC for electric vehicles. Specifies all required safety tests for EV approval.

components.body:
You have completed the EV Awareness & Safety course. Remember: always identify the vehicle type before beginning any work, never touch orange cables, and follow your organisation's specific EV safety procedures.

If you have any questions about working with electric vehicles, consult your workshop manager or a suitably trained EV specialist.
